subject: Quick question on transition workflows
email_text:
Hi James,

I have been following your work at IBM, and I wanted to follow up on my earlier note about CFO transitions.

For CFO leaders at IBM, the real risk is sounding competent but not board-ready when the first serious conversation starts.

In recent CFO-track transitions, first conversations improved when board language and capital allocation logic were explicit from minute one.

For CFO candidates, that is where faster translation of finance credibility into board-readable transition narratives usually shows up in the first serious conversation.

If this is not relevant right now, no worries.

If useful, I can send a one-page sample built around your CFO context.

If useful, If helpful, I can send a one-page example. If not useful right now, no worries.

Rich

email_text:
Hi Libby,

I have been following your work at PIMCO, and I wanted to follow up on my earlier note about COO transitions.

For COO leaders at PIMCO, the real risk is being known for execution without proving the scale and sequencing that the next role requires.

In recent COO-track transitions, conversion improved when execution stories included sequencing decisions and operating leverage proof.

For COO candidates, that is where stronger execution-cadence proof in early outreach and screening calls usually shows up in the first serious conversation.

I have been following your work at Sierra, and I wanted to follow up on my earlier note about CTO transitions.

For CTO leaders at Sierra, the real risk is being seen as technically strong but too abstract for a CEO, board, or investor audience.
